input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin system** being studied is not explicitly defined in the provided document. However, the context suggests a **complex engineered system** (e.g., manufacturing, industrial, or cyber-physical system) where **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies are applied. Key characteristics include:
  - **Multi-layered abstraction**: The system is modeled across **requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers** (e.g., via **RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, or SMArDT** methodologies).
  - **Component-based structure**: Described using **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME, AADL, MontiArc, or SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)**, emphasizing **components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition**.
  - **Behavioral modeling**: Incorporates **state machines (SysML v1.7), MontiArc Automaton, or simulation tools** (e.g., **MathWorks Simulink**) for dynamic behavior representation.
  - **Heterogeneous stakeholder integration**: Requires consolidation of **diverse modeling notations and tools** to address **multi-perspective system complexity**.
  - **Digital Shadow integration**: The system is implicitly linked to a **Digital Shadow** (as per Kritzinger et al.), characterized by **one-way automated data flow** from the physical object to a digital representation, with **passive data aggregation** (e.g., contextual traces for manufacturing).
  - **Operational domain**: Likely **manufacturing or industrial systems** (inferred from the Digital Shadow reference model in **Fig. 1**), where real-time data collection and abstraction are critical for digital twinning.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** provides **active services** to complement the **passive Digital Shadow** (as per Kritzinger et al.), enabling:
  - **Data Interpretation & Processing**: Acts as an **active component** to **interpret and process incoming data** from the physical system (unlike the Digital Shadow, which is one-way and passive).
  - **Functional & Logical Architecture Services**:
    - **Component-Connector Analysis**: Leverages **ADLs (e.g., ACME, AADL, MontiArc)** to model **interfaces, interconnections, and composition** of system components.
    - **Behavioral Simulation**: Integrates **state machines (SysML v1.7), MontiArc Automaton, or simulation tools (e.g., Simulink)** for **dynamic behavior modeling and validation**.
  - **Target Users**:
    - **System Engineers**: For **MBSE-driven design, validation, and optimization** across **requirements, functional, and technical layers**.
    - **Operational Stakeholders**: For **real-time monitoring, predictive analytics, and control** (implied by the DT’s active role in processing data).
  - **Service Capabilities**:
    - **Monitoring**: Aggregates and abstracts **contextual data traces** (e.g., from sensors or logs) for **real-time system state awareness**.
    - **Optimization**: Enables **multi-perspective analysis** (via **heterogeneous modeling tools**) to refine system performance.
    - **Prediction**: Supports **simulation-based forecasting** (e.g., using **Simulink or other simulation engines**) for proactive decision-making.
    - **Control**: Provides **feedback mechanisms** (unlike the Digital Shadow) to **adjust physical system behavior** based on digital insights.
    - **Visualization**: Facilitates **integrated views** of **multi-layered models** (e.g., combining **SysML, ADLs, and simulation outputs**) for stakeholder alignment.
  - **Not in Document**: Specific **control algorithms, optimization metrics, or user interfaces** are not detailed.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin implementation** relies on a **heterogeneous toolchain** combining:
  - **Modeling & Architecture Tools**:
    - **SysML (v1.7)**: For **systems engineering modeling**, including **requirements, functional/logical architectures, and state machines**.
    - **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)**:
      - **ACME**: Component-based system modeling.
      - **AADL**: Architecture-centric modeling for **safety-critical systems**.
      - **MontiArc**: Supports **component composition, interfaces, and behavioral automata**.
      - **SysML IBDs**: For **logical and physical architecture visualization**.
  - **Simulation & Behavioral Modeling**:
    - **MathWorks Simulink**: For **dynamic system simulation** and **control system design**.
    - **Other Simulation Tools/Methods**: Referenced generically (e.g., [29], [30]) for **multi-domain behavioral analysis**.
  - **General-Purpose Programming**:
    - **C++/Java**: For **custom logic, algorithms, or integration layers** between modeling tools.
  - **Data & Integration Enablers**:
    - **Digital Shadow Framework**: Provides **one-way data flow** from physical to digital (as per **Fig. 1**), with **contextual trace aggregation** (e.g., for manufacturing).
    - **MBSE Methodologies**: **RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT** to **structure modeling complexity** across layers.
  - **Not in Document**:
    - **Specific databases, IoT protocols, or cloud platforms** are not mentioned.
    - **Real-time data acquisition systems** (e.g., PLCs, SCADA) are implied but not detailed.
    - **AI/ML frameworks** (e.g., for predictive analytics) are not referenced.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system may involve **multiple instances** of digital representations, particularly in decentralized architectures. The provided context does not specify exact multiplicities (e.g., 1:1, 1:N, or N:M mappings between physical and digital components), but it implies a **hierarchical structure** where digital twins can be instantiated at different levels of abstraction (e.g., requirements, functional, logical, or product/technical layers). The **centralized vs. decentralized** architecture is not explicitly defined, though the integration of heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives (as mentioned in MBSE) suggests potential **distributed coordination mechanisms**. The **scope of responsibilities** for each instance would depend on the system's modeling methodology (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM, or SMArDT), which partitions the system into modular components with defined interfaces and interconnections (e.g., via ADLs like ACME, AADL, or SysML IBDs). No explicit mention of synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity is provided in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The **Digital Twin constellation** in this context is implicitly tied to **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, where the system is decomposed into **multi-layered abstractions** (requirements, functional, logical, and product/technical layers). Orchestration and coordination are achieved through **architecture description languages (ADLs)** such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, or SysML IBDs, which define **component & connector perspectives**, including interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavioral coordination is further refined using **state machines (SysML v1.7)** or **MontiArc Automaton**, while detailed behavior is modeled via **simulation tools (e.g., MathWorks Simulink)** or **general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java)**. The **system-wide coordination** relies on integrating these heterogeneous models and tools, often requiring **active components** (as opposed to passive digital shadows) to interpret and process data flows. No explicit architecture patterns (e.g., microservices, service-oriented) or resource management strategies are detailed, but the emphasis on **stakeholder integration** and **multi-perspective modeling** suggests a **modular, component-based orchestration** framework.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The **horizontal integration** of the Digital Twin system is not explicitly detailed in the provided document. However, the context implies dependencies on **external systems** through the use of **heterogeneous modeling tools and notations** (e.g., SysML, ADLs, Simulink, C++, Java). Interoperability is inferred from the need to **integrate multiple stakeholders and perspectives**, which may require **data exchange protocols** or **interoperability standards** (not specified). The document does not mention **concrete integration patterns** (e.g., APIs, message brokers, event-driven architectures) or **external system dependencies** (e.g., ERP, MES, or IoT platforms). While the Digital Twin may interact with other Digital Twins or enterprise systems, no technical details (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT, REST) or **enterprise integration frameworks** are provided. Thus, the horizontal integration remains **undefined in the document**.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin (DT) system leverages **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies to integrate heterogeneous computational models and data components across multiple abstraction layers (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers). Key model types include **geometric models** (e.g., CAD representations), **behavioral models** (e.g., state machines in SysML v1.7 or MontiArc Automaton), and **analytical models** (e.g., simulations in MathWorks Simulink or other simulation tools like those referenced in [29], [30]). Data sources are derived from **physical systems** (e.g., sensors, actuators) and **external systems** (e.g., enterprise data, third-party APIs), with data management approaches involving **architecture description languages (ADLs)** such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, or SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs) to define components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Model relationships are established via **formalized interfaces** (e.g., connectors in ADLs) and **tool-specific integrations** (e.g., co-simulation between Simulink and ADLs). The DT constellation aggregates these models and data to enable **real-time synchronization** and **multi-perspective analysis**, addressing stakeholder-specific requirements through methodologies like RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) or OOSEM. Data traces and abstractions are collected as part of the **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (per [32]), where contextual data is passively acquired and aggregated for specific purposes, later processed by active DT components for autonomous interpretation and feedback.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The Digital Twin system incorporates **validation and verification (V&V) methods** to ensure model fidelity, though specific techniques are not detailed in the provided document. Fidelity requirements are implicitly tied to the **abstraction layers** of MBSE (e.g., logical vs. physical models) and the **integration of heterogeneous tools** (e.g., ADLs, Simulink, general-purpose languages like C++/Java), which may introduce **uncertainty sources** such as data latency, model discretization errors, or tool-specific approximations. Error detection and correction mechanisms are not explicitly described, but the system relies on **active components** (e.g., DT-specific software) to interpret and process incoming data from the **Digital Shadow**, as referenced in [35]–[37]. Quality assurance processes are inferred to involve **cross-tool consistency checks** (e.g., validating ADL models against simulation outputs) and **stakeholder-specific perspectives**, though no quantitative metrics (e.g., uncertainty quantification, precision thresholds) are provided.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in a Digital Twin (DT) system is characterized by an **automated one-way data flow** from the physical object to its digital representation, as defined by Kritzinger et al. [32]. This flow involves **contextual data traces** collected from sensors or IoT devices, which are aggregated, abstracted, and stored for specific analytical or monitoring purposes. The data types transmitted may include **real-time sensor readings** (e.g., temperature, pressure, vibration) or **operational logs** (e.g., equipment states, performance metrics). The communication protocols are not explicitly specified in the document, but typical implementations may rely on **industrial protocols** (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT, AMQP) or **standardized IoT protocols** (e.g., CoAP, HTTP/REST). Transmission frequencies depend on the application but are often **event-triggered** (e.g., threshold breaches, state changes) or **time-based** (e.g., periodic polling). Data quality assurance mechanisms may include **preprocessing steps** such as filtering, normalization, or validation against expected ranges, though specific techniques are not detailed. Integration mechanisms involve **heterogeneous data aggregation** (e.g., combining structured sensor data with unstructured logs) and **abstraction layers** to derive higher-level insights, often leveraging **model-based systems engineering (MBSE) tools** like SysML or simulation environments (e.g., MathWorks Simulink) for further analysis.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The **technical network infrastructure** for a Digital Twin (DT) system is not explicitly detailed in the provided document. However, the **one-way data flow** of a digital shadow (as a foundational concept for DTs) implies that the network must support **reliable, low-latency communication** between physical assets and digital representations. While specific protocols (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT, TCP/IP) or topologies (e.g., star, mesh) are not mentioned, the infrastructure must accommodate **heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives** [31], suggesting a **scalable, modular architecture**. Security protocols (e.g., encryption, authentication) are implied but not specified, as is redundancy (e.g., failover mechanisms) or bandwidth requirements. The document does not provide details on latency constraints or network architecture (e.g., cloud-edge hybrid).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The Digital Twin (DT) engineering and development process leverages **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, utilizing systems engineering modeling languages such as **SysML** for structuring system descriptions across multiple abstraction layers (e.g., requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers). Methodologies like **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM**, **SPES**, and **SMArDT** are employed to manage complexity. For functional and logical architecture, **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME**, **AADL**, **MontiArc**, and **SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** are used to define components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavioral modeling is enhanced with tools like **MontiArc Automaton** or **SysML state machines**, often integrated with simulation tools (e.g., **MathWorks Simulink**) or general-purpose programming languages (e.g., **C++**, **Java**). The process involves heterogeneous stakeholder integration and tool interoperability to ensure comprehensive system representation and evolution. **Quality assurance** is implicitly tied to the use of standardized modeling languages and methodologies, while **version control** and **evolution management** are not explicitly detailed in the provided context. The transition between lifecycle phases (e.g., development to operational use) is governed by the structured modeling approaches and tool integrations described, ensuring traceability and consistency across phases.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system adheres to **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** standards, specifically leveraging **SysML** (Systems Modeling Language) for system modeling across abstraction layers. For architecture description, it complies with **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME**, **AADL**, **MontiArc**, and **SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)**. Behavioral modeling aligns with **SysML state machines** (v1.7+) and **MontiArc Automaton**. Interoperability is facilitated through integration with simulation tools like **MathWorks Simulink** and general-purpose programming languages (**C++**, **Java**). While no explicit certification frameworks or compliance standards (e.g., ISO, IEC) are mentioned, the use of these standardized modeling languages ensures alignment with industry best practices for system description, interoperability, and modularity. The **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (as per Kritzinger et al.) implicitly supports standardization by defining structured data aggregation and abstraction for manufacturing contexts.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Digital Twin system, as described in the context documents, integrates **cybersecurity protocols** and **access control mechanisms** through the **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** framework (referenced in [46], [47], and [50]). The AAS standardizes data encapsulation and exchange, enabling role-based access control (RBAC) and authentication systems to ensure secure interactions between physical assets and their digital representations. Additionally, the **RAMI4.0 reference architecture** ([48], [49]) incorporates hierarchical security layers aligned with the **automation pyramid**, where each level (e.g., field, station, enterprise) enforces granular access policies and threat mitigation strategies. The **MQTT protocol** ([52]), utilized for lightweight IoT communication, supports TLS/SSL encryption to secure data transmission between Digital Twin components and edge devices. **Fail-safe mechanisms** are implied in model-driven engineering approaches ([43]), where simulation models (e.g., Functional Mockup Interface - FMI ([51])) validate system behavior under fault conditions. Risk assessment is implicitly tied to **conformance relations** ([41]) for structural validation of Digital Twin models, ensuring compliance with safety-critical constraints. However, explicit details on **consent management**, **data anonymization**, or **compliance with regulations** (e.g., GDPR) are not provided in the documents.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

[41] M. Konersmann, J. Michael, and B. Rumpe, “Towards Reference Models
with Conformance Relations for Structure,” in _Informing Possible Future_
_Worlds. Essays in Honour of Ulrich Frank_ . Logos Verlag Berlin, 2024,
pp. 247–269.

[42] M. Heithoff, C. Hopmann, T. Köbel, J. Michael, B. Rumpe, and P. Sapel,
“Application of Digital Shadows on Different Levels in the Automation
Pyramid,” _Data and Knowledge Engineering (DKE)_, vol. 158, 2025.

[43] M. Heithoff, J. Michael, B. Rumpe, J. Pfeiffer, A. Wortmann, and
J. Zhang, “A Method for Model-Driven Engineering of Digital Twins
in Manufacturing,” in _28th ACM/IEEE Int. Conf. on Model Driven_
_Engineering Languages and Systems_ . IEEE, 2025.

[44] MABI, “Mabi robot,” [https://www.mabi-robotic.com/en/robotic/](https://www.mabi-robotic.com/en/robotic/robotic-arm/max-100-2-25-p)

“Friction modeling for structured learning of robot dynamics,” in _Lecture_
